Ayahs

نٓ ۚ وَٱلْقَلَمِ وَمَا يَسْطُرُونَ (١)
Nun. By the Pen and what they write: (1)
مَآ أَنتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ (٢)
by your Lord’s blessing, you are not, crazy, (2)
وَإِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْرًا غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ (٣)
and yours indeed will be an everlasting reward, (3)
وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ (٤)
and indeed you possess a great character. (4)
فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَيُبْصِرُونَ (٥)
You will see and they will see, (5)
بِأَييِّكُمُ ٱلْمَفْتُونُ (٦)
which one of you is crazy. (6)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٧)
Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided. (7)

Content of Surah al-Qalam

Although some commentators have doubted whether the entire Surah is "Makki," or believe that part of it was revealed in "Medina" and part in "Mecca," the tone of the Surah and the content of the verses are completely consistent with "Makki" Surahs. This is because it revolves primarily around the issue of the Prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the struggle against the enemies who called him insane, the call to patience and steadfastness, and the warning and threatening of the opponents with Divine punishment.

Overall, the topics of this Surah can be summarized into seven sections:

  1. First, it mentions some of the special attributes of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, especially his outstanding character, and emphasizes this with emphatic sections.

  2. Then, it recounts some of the ugly traits and reprehensible morals of his enemies.

  3. In another section, the story of the "Owners of the Garden" (أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ) is narrated, which is in fact a warning to the evil-natured polytheists.

  4. In another part, various matters regarding the Resurrection and the punishment of the disbelievers on that Day are mentioned.

  5. In another section, warnings and threats directed at the polytheists are recounted.

  6. In another part, it commands the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to show steadfastness and patience against the stubborn enemies.

  7. Finally, at the end of the Surah, it speaks of the greatness of the Quran and the various conspiracies of the enemies against the Prophet.

The name "al-Qalam" (The Pen) was chosen for this Surah in accordance with its first verse. Some have also referred to it as Surah "Nun," and it can be inferred from some narrations regarding the virtue of this Surah that its name is Surah "Nun wal-Qalam."

Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Qalam

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

من قرأ سورة "ن و القلم" اعطاه اللَّه ثواب الذين حسن اخلاقهم:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Nun wal-Qalam,' Allah will grant him the reward of those who possess good character." [1] And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام we read:

من قرأ سورة "ن و القلم" فى فريضة او نافلة آمنه اللَّه ان يصيبه فى حياته فقر أبدا، و اعاذه اذا مات من ضمة القبر ان شاء اللَّه:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Nun wal-Qalam' in an obligatory or supererogatory prayer, Allah will secure him from poverty forever during his life, and when he dies, He will grant him refuge from the pressure of the grave, if Allah wills." [2] These rewards have a special proportion to the content of the Surah and indicate that the goal is a recitation accompanied by awareness followed by action.

[1]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 387. [2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 330.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

ن وَ الْقَلَمِ وَ ما يَسْطُرُونَ (1) ما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ (2) وَ إِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْراً غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ (3) وَ إِنَّكَ لَعَلى‌ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ (4)

فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَ يُبْصِرُونَ (5) بِأَيِّكُمُ الْمَفْتُونُ (6) إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ (7)

Commentary

What Outstanding Character You Possess!

This is the only Surah that begins with the abbreviated letter "Nun" (ن). It says: (Nun). Regarding the interpretation of the abbreviated letters (Muqatta'at), we have discussed them many times, especially at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and al-A'raf (Volumes 1, 2, and 6). What must be added here is that some have considered "Nun" here to be an abbreviation of the word "Rahman" (The All-Merciful) and an allusion to it. Others have interpreted it to mean "The Tablet" (Lawh), or "The Inkwell," or a "River" in Paradise. However, none of these interpretations have a clear context or evidence.

Therefore, the interpretation of this abbreviated letter is not separate from the interpretation of all the abbreviated letters mentioned above.

Then, He swears by two of the most important matters of human life, adding: By the "Pen" and what they write with the pen: (وَ الْقَلَمِ وَ ما يَسْطُرُونَ).

What an amazing oath! In reality, that which is sworn by here is apparently a small object: a piece of reed or something similar, and a bit of black substance, and then lines inscribed upon a meager page of paper.

But in reality, this is the very thing that is the source of the emergence of all human civilizations, the progress and perfection of sciences, the awakening of thoughts and ideas, the formation of religions, and the source of human guidance and awareness—to the extent that it divides the era of human life into two periods: "the historical era" and "the pre-historic era." The historical era of man begins from the time writing was invented and man was able to record the story of his life on pages; or in other words, it is the era when man took up the pen and "what they write" (ما يَسْطُرُونَ) remained as a legacy.

The greatness of this oath becomes more apparent when we consider that on the day these verses were revealed, there were no writers or masters of the pen in that environment. If there were people with a meager literacy of reading and writing, their number in the entire land of Mecca—which was the religious, political, and economic center of the Hejaz—did not reach twenty people. Yes, swearing by the pen in such an environment carries a special greatness.

Interestingly, in the first verses revealed to the pure heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in "Jabal al-Nur" and the cave of "Hira," the high status of the pen was also mentioned, where He says:

اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ- خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ- اقْرَأْ وَ رَبُّكَ الْأَكْرَمُ- الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ- عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ‌:

"Read in the name of your Lord who created—created man from a clinging clot. Read, and your Lord is the most Generous—who taught by the pen—taught man that which he knew not" (Surah al-Alaq 1-5).

And most interesting of all is that all these words flow from the tongue of one who himself was unschooled, never went to a traditional school, and never wrote; this too is proof that it is nothing but Divine revelation.

Some commentators have interpreted "the Pen" here as the pen with which the great angels of God write the Divine revelation, or with which they record the deeds of humans. However, the verse certainly has a broad meaning, of which this interpretation is one instance, just as "what they write" (ما يَسْطُرُونَ) also has a vast meaning, encompassing everything written in the path of human guidance and intellectual, moral, and practical perfection, and is not limited to Divine revelation or human deeds. [1]

Then, He addresses the subject for which the oath was taken, saying: "By the grace of your Lord, you are not insane" (ما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ). Those who attribute this false accusation to you are the blind-hearted who do not see all these Divine blessings upon you: the blessing of abundant intellect and insight, the blessing of trustworthiness, sincerity, and truthfulness, and the blessing of manifest knowledge, Prophethood, and the station of infallibility.

The insane are those who accuse the manifestation of Universal Intellect of insanity, and distance themselves from the leader and guide of humanity with this false attribution.

Following that, He adds: "And indeed, for you is a reward great and unending" (وَ إِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْراً غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ). Why should you not have such a reward? While you remain steadfast against these ugly and false accusations, and you wish for their guidance and salvation, and you never tire of striving and effort in this path.

"Mamnun" is derived from the root "mann," meaning "interruption." That is, a reward and recompense that is never cut off and remains permanently. Some have said the root of this meaning is taken from "minnah" (favor/obligation), because reminding one of a favor causes the blessing to be cut off.

Some have also said that "ghayru mamnun" means that Allah will never place you under an obligation (minnah) for this great reward, but the first interpretation is more appropriate.

The next verse, in another description of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, says: "And indeed, you are of a great and outstanding character" (وَ إِنَّكَ لَعَلى‌ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ). A character at which the intellect is bewildered: an unparalleled kindness and love, a matchless purity and sincerity, and an indescribable patience, steadfastness, endurance, and tolerance.

If you call people to the servitude of Allah, you yourself worship more than anyone else; and if you forbid an evil deed, you yourself abstain before anyone else. They harm you and you advise them; they insult you and you pray for them; they pelt your body with stones and pour hot ashes on your head, and you raise your hands to the threshold of Allah for their guidance.

Yes, you are the center of love and affection and the source of mercy.

"Khuluq" (character) is from the root "khilqat" (creation), meaning attributes that do not separate from a person and become like the creation and nature of the human being.

Some commentators have interpreted the "great character" of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as "patience in the way of truth, extensive generosity and giving, management of affairs, gentleness and leniency, enduring hardships in the path of calling towards Allah, forgiveness and pardon, Jihad in the way of the Lord, and abandoning envy and greed." However, although all these attributes were in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, his "great character" was not limited to these.

In some commentaries, "great character" has also been interpreted as the Quran or the religion of Islam, which can be among the instances of the broad concept above. In any case, the existence of this "great character" in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was a clear proof of his intellect and insight and a negation of the enemies' accusations.

Following that, He adds: "Soon you will see, and they will see" (فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَ يُبْصِرُونَ).

"Which of you is the afflicted one (the insane)"?! (بِأَيِّكُمُ الْمَفْتُونُ). [1] "Maftun" is the passive participle from "fitnah," meaning affliction, and here it means being afflicted with insanity.

[1]: The "ba" in "bi-ayyukum" is extra (za'idah), and "ayyukum" is the object for the two preceding verbs.

Yes, today they give you this false attribution to distance the servants of Allah from you, but people possess intellect and consciousness; they will gradually become aware of your teachings and words. At that time, it will become clear that these outstanding teachings were revealed from the Great Lord upon your pure and luminous heart, and that Allah has granted you a vast portion of intellect and knowledge.

Your movements and stances in the future, and the rapid progress and influence of Islam in their shadow, will also show that you are a great source of intellect and insight. The insane are the bats who rose to fight the light of this sun.

And of course, in the Resurrection, these truths will become even clearer and more manifest.

Again, for further emphasis, He says: "Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ). For the path is His path, and He knows His path better than anyone. In this way, He gives more assurance to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he is on the path of guidance and his enemies are on the path of misguidance.

In a documented hadith, it is stated that when the Quraysh saw the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله preferring Ali عليه السلام over others and honoring him, they began to disparage Ali عليه السلام and said: "Muhammad has been infatuated (maftun) by him." It was then that Allah revealed "Nun wal-Qalam" and swore by it that: O Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, you are not infatuated or insane—until He said—Allah knows those who have gone astray, referring to the group of Quraysh who said those words, and Allah knows the guided ones better, referring to Ali عليه السلام. [1]

Key Points

1. The Role of the Pen in Human Life

One of the most important events in human life—as previously mentioned—was the emergence of writing and the movement of the pen across pages or stones; it was this that separated the historical era from the pre-historic.

The movement of the pen's nib on the page of paper records the destiny of humanity; therefore, the victory and defeat of human societies are tied to the tips of pens.

The "Pen" is the preserver of sciences and knowledge, the guardian of the thoughts of thinkers, the intellectual link between scholars, and the bridge connecting the past and future of humanity. Even the connection between heaven and earth has been achieved through the Tablet and the Pen! The pen links human beings who live apart from each other in terms of time and place; it is as if you see all the thinkers of humanity throughout history and across the entire face of the earth gathered in one large library! The "Pen" is the keeper of man's secrets, the treasurer of sciences, and the collector of the experiences of centuries and ages. If the Quran swears by it, it is for this reason, because an oath is always taken by a very great and valuable matter.

And of course, the "Pen" is a tool for "what they write" (ما يَسْطُرُونَ) and the writings, both of which the Quran has sworn by: both the "tool" and the "product" of the tool.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 334 (Tabarsi narrated this hadith with his chain from Sunni sources).

In some narrations, it is stated that:

ان اول ما خلق اللَّه القلم‌

"The first thing Allah created was the Pen."

The Shia traditionists have narrated this hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [1] and it also appears in Sunni books as a well-known report. [2] In another hadith, it says:

اول ما خلق اللَّه تعالى جوهرة

"The first thing Allah the Exalted created was a jewel." 3 And in some reports, it is stated:

ان اول ما خلق اللَّه العقل‌

"The first thing Allah created was the Intellect." 4 Attention to the special link between the "Jewel," the "Pen," and the "Intellect" clarifies the concept of all of them being "first."

In the continuation of the hadith narrated above from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that after creating the pen, Allah said to it: "Write!" and it wrote what has been and what will be until the Day of Resurrection! Although the pen in this narration refers to the pen of destiny and Divine decree, whatever it may be, it clarifies the role of the pen in the destiny of man and his predestinations.

The leaders of Islam, in numerous hadiths, emphasized to their companions not to rely solely on their memory, but to record Islamic hadiths and Divine sciences in writing and leave them as a legacy for future generations. [5] Some scholars have said: "Expression (al-Bayan) is of two types: expression of the tongue and expression of the fingertips (the pen). The expression of the tongue is worn away by the years, but the expression of the pens remains over the passage of days!"

[1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 389, Hadith 9. [2] & 3: Tafsir "Fakhr al-Razi," Vol. 30, p. 78.

[5]: "Wasa'il al-Shia," Vol. 18, p. 56 (Hadiths 14, 16, 17, 18, 19, and 20).

"The foundation of the affairs of religion and the world rests on two things: the Pen and the Sword, and the sword is under the shadow of the pen!" [2] Some Arabic poets have versified this meaning as follows:

"Thus Allah decreed for the pens since they were sharpened, that the swords, since they were honed, should be their servants!" (This expression is a subtle allusion to the sharpening of the pen with a knife and the placement of sharp blades in the service of the pen from the very beginning). 3 Another poet, citing the verses under discussion, says:

"If the heroes one day swear by their swords, and count them among what brings glory and nobility, It is enough pride and elevation for the pen of the writers throughout time that Allah swore by the Pen (and not by the sword)!" 4

[1] & [2]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 332.

4: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 102.

And truly it is so, for military victories will never be lasting if they are not guaranteed by a powerful culture. The Mongols achieved the greatest victory in Iranian history, but because they were a nation without culture, they were soon dissolved into the culture of Islam and Iran and changed their course.

Although this discussion is very extensive, in order not to deviate from the method of commentary, we conclude the speech with a very meaningful hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard:

ثلاث تخرق الحجب، و تنتهى الى ما بين يدى اللَّه: صرير اقلام العلماء، و وطى اقدام المجاهدين، و صوت مغازل المحصنات‌

"There are three sounds that pierce the veils and reach the presence of Allah's majesty: the scratching of the pens of scholars while writing, the sound of the footsteps of mujahideen in the field of Jihad, and the sound of the spinning wheels of chaste women!" [1] Of course, all that has been said is regarding pens that move in the path of truth and justice and on the straight path; however, poisonous and misleading pens are considered the greatest calamity and the most immense danger for human societies.

2. An Example of the Prophet's (ص) Character

The victory of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, although supported by Divine aid and assistance, had many factors from an outward perspective, one of the most important of which was the moral attraction of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Such high human qualities and noble traits were gathered in him that they influenced stubborn enemies and forced them to surrender, and deeply attracted friends.

Rather, if we call this the moral miracle of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we have not exaggerated, just as an example of this moral miracle became manifest in the Conquest of Mecca: when the bloodthirsty and criminal polytheists—who for many years had used everything in their power against Islam and the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—were caught in the hands of the Muslims, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, contrary to all calculations of friends and enemies, issued a decree of general amnesty for them and consigned all their crimes to oblivion. This became the reason that, according to the verse "they enter the religion of Allah in crowds," they became Muslim in droves.

Regarding the good character of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his pardon, forgiveness, affection, kindness, altruism, sacrifice, and piety, many stories have appeared in books of commentary and history, all of which would take us out of the scope of commentary. However, we must say this much: in a hadith from Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام, it is stated that he said:

I asked my father, the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, about the characteristics of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life and his character, and my father answered me in detail. In part of this hadith, it says:

The Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله behavior with his companions was such that he was always cheerful, smiling, easy-going, and gentle. He was never harsh, hard-hearted, aggressive, foul-mouthed, fault-finding, or a flatterer. No one despaired of him, and whoever came to his door did not return disappointed. He had abandoned three things from himself: arguing in speech, talkativeness, and interfering in what did not concern him. And he had abandoned three things regarding people: he did not disparage anyone, he did not reproach anyone, and he did not search for the slips and hidden faults of people.

He never spoke except in matters where he hoped for Divine reward. When he spoke, his words were so influential that everyone remained silent and did not move, and when he became silent, they would speak, but they never quarreled or argued in his presence... Whenever a stranger or an ignorant person spoke harshly and made a request, he would endure it and say to his companions: "Whenever you see someone in need, grant it to him." And he never interrupted anyone's speech until it ended. [1] Yes, if it were not for this noble character and these virtuous traits, that backward ignorant nation and that harsh, inflexible group would not have embraced Islam, and according to the verse "they would have dispersed from around you," they would all have scattered.

And how good it would be if this Islamic character were revived today and a reflection of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله character were in every Muslim.

Islamic narrations in this regard, both concerning the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the duty of all Muslims, are abundant. Here we mention a few narrations:

  1. In a hadith, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

انما بعثت لاتمم مكارم الاخلاق‌

"I was only sent to complete the noble traits of character." [2] Thus, one of the primary goals of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission is this completion of virtuous character.

  1. In another hadith from him, it says:

انما المؤمن ليدرك بحسن خلقه درجة قائم الليل و صائم النهار

"Indeed, the believer, through his good character, reaches the rank of one who stands in prayer at night and fasts during the day." 3

[1]: "Ma'ani al-Akhbar," p. 83 (with slight abridgment). [2] & 3: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 333.

  1. And again from him, he said:

ما من شى‌ء اثقل فى الميزان من خلق حسن‌

"There is nothing heavier in the balance of deeds on the Day of Resurrection than good character." [1]

  1. It is also narrated from him that he said:

احبكم الى اللَّه احسنكم اخلاقا الموطؤن اكنافا، الذين يالفون و يؤلفون، و ابغضكم الى اللَّه المشاؤن بالنميمة، المفرقون بين الاخوان، الملتمسون للبرءاء العثرات:

"The most beloved of you to Allah is the one whose character is the best, those who are humble, who get along with others and others get along with them. And the most hated of you to Allah are the tale-bearers, those who cause separation between brothers, and those who seek slips in innocent people." [2]

  1. In another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

اكثر ما يدخل الناس الجنة تقوى اللَّه و حسن الخلق‌

"The things that most commonly enter people into Paradise are the consciousness of Allah (Taqwa) and good character." 3

  1. In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it says:

ان اكمل المؤمنين ايمانا احسنهم خلقا

"Among the believers, the one whose faith is most complete is the one whose character is the best." 4

  1. In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

عليكم بحسن الخلق، فان حسن الخلق فى الجنة لا محالة، و اياكم و سوء الخلق فان سوء الخلق فى النار لا محالة:

"It is incumbent upon you to seek good character, for good character is inevitably in Paradise; and beware of bad character, for bad character is inevitably in the Fire." [5]

From the sum of the above reports, it is well understood that good character is the key to Paradise, a means of drawing Allah's pleasure, a sign of the strength of faith, and on par with day and night worship. Hadiths in this regard are very numerous.

[1] & [2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 333. 3: "Safinat al-Bihar," Vol. 1, p. 410 (the same meaning appears in Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 8, p. 504, and also in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6707). 4: "Wasa'il al-Shia," Vol. 8, p. 506, Hadith 21. [5]: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 108.

Ayahs

فَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٨)
So do not obey the deniers, (8)
وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ (٩)
who are eager that you should be flexible, so that they [too] may be flexible [towards you]. (9)
وَلَا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَّهِينٍ (١٠)
And do not obey any vile swearer, (10)
هَمَّازٍ مَّشَّآءٍۭ بِنَمِيمٍ (١١)
scandal-monger, talebearer, (11)
مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ (١٢)
hinderer of all good, sinful transgressor, (12)
عُتُلٍّۭ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ زَنِيمٍ (١٣)
callous and, on top of that, baseborn (13)
أَن كَانَ ذَا مَالٍ وَبَنِينَ (١٤)
—[who behaves in such a manner only] because he has wealth and children. (14)
إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٥)
When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’ (15)
سَنَسِمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلْخُرْطُومِ (١٦)
Soon We shall brand him on his snout. (16)

Commentary

Do Not Follow Those Who Possess These Traits

After mentioning the sublime character of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the preceding verses, these verses proceed to describe the character of his enemies so that, through comparison, the distance between the two becomes perfectly clear.

First, He says: "So do not obey the deniers" (فَلا تُطِعِ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ)—those who deny Allah, the Prophet, the Day of Resurrection, and His religion.

They are a misguided and seductive people who have trampled upon all principles of truth; obeying such individuals, even in a single word, results in nothing but deviation and misery.

Then, referring to their efforts to draw the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) into compromise, He adds: "They wish that you would soften [your stance], so they [too] would soften" (وَدُّوا لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ). "Softening" and flexibility here mean abandoning part of Allah's commands for their sake.

Commentators have narrated that these verses were revealed when the leaders of Mecca invited the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to follow the religion of their ancestors, polytheism, and idol-worship; Allah forbade him from obeying them [1]. Others have narrated that "Walid ibn al-Mughirah," who was among the great leaders of polytheism, offered vast wealth to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and swore that if he returned from his religion, he would give it to him! [2] From the tone of the verses and historical accounts, it is clearly understood that when the blind-hearted polytheists observed the rapid progress of Islam, they thought of gaining concessions from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by offering him concessions—drawing him into a kind of compromise, as is the method of all followers of falsehood throughout history. Thus, they sometimes offered immense wealth, sometimes beautiful women, and sometimes prominent positions and status; in reality, they were measuring and comparing the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) spirit by the scale of their own existence.

However, the Quran repeatedly warned the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) never to show the slightest flexibility toward these deviant proposals and never to compromise (mudahanah) with the people of falsehood. As we read in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 49: (وَ أَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ وَ احْذَرْهُمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنُوكَ عَنْ بَعْضِ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ): "And judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations and beware of them, lest they tempt you away from some of what Allah has revealed to you."

"Yudhinun" is derived from the root mudahanah, which originally comes from duhn, meaning "oil" or "grease." In such contexts, it means to show softness and flexibility. Usually, this expression is used for blameworthy and hypocritical flexibility.

Then, He once again forbids obeying them and enumerates nine blameworthy traits, any one of which alone could be a barrier to obedience and following. He says: "And do not obey every habitual swearer, [who is] despicable" (وَ لا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَهِينٍ).

"Hallaf" refers to someone who swears excessively, taking an oath for every small or large matter; usually, such individuals are not truthful in their oaths.

"Mahin" is derived from mahanah, meaning meanness and despicability. Some have interpreted it to mean those of weak intellect, liars, or the wicked.

Then He adds: "[A] slanderer, proceeding with detraction" (هَمَّازٍ مَشَّاءٍ بِنَمِيمٍ). "Hammaz" is from the root hamz, meaning backbiting and fault-finding.

"Mashsha'in bi-namim" is one who goes about to disrupt and cause corruption among people and to create hostility and enmity. (It should be noted that both descriptions appear in the intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah), indicating their extreme persistence in these ugly acts).

In the fifth, sixth, and seventh descriptions, He says: "[A] preventer of good, transgressing and sinful" (مَنَّاعٍ لِلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ). Not only does he not perform good deeds himself or point toward the path of goodness, but he is a barrier against the good and blessings of others. Furthermore, he is a transgressor of Divine limits and the rights Allah has established for every human being. In addition to these traits, he is tainted with every kind of sin, such that sin has become part of his nature.

Finally, referring to their eighth and ninth traits, He says: "Cruel, and moreover, an ignoble pretender" (عُتُلٍّ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ زَنِيمٍ). "Utull," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, refers to someone who eats excessively, pulls everything toward himself, and prevents others from it.

Others have interpreted "Utull" as an ill-tempered, vengeful, and harsh person, or a shameless and foul-mannered individual.

"Zanim" is someone whose origin and lineage are unclear, attributed to a people while not being of them. Originally, it comes from zanamah, referring to a piece of a sheep's ear that hangs down, as if it is not part of the ear but merely attached to it.

The expression "after that" (ba'da dhalika) indicates that these two traits are even uglier and more reprehensible than the previous ones, as a group of commentators have inferred.

In short, Allah has provided such a depiction of the deniers and their ugly traits and vile morals here that perhaps no parallel exists in the entire Quran. In this way, He clarifies what kind of individuals the opponents of Islam, the Quran, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) were: liars, despicable, fault-finders, slanders, sinful transgressors, and of ignoble birth. Truly, opposition to such a great reformer is not expected from anyone other than such individuals.

In the next verse, He warns: "Lest, because he possesses wealth and children [you should yield to him]" (أَنْ كانَ ذا مالٍ وَ بَنِينَ). Undoubtedly, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would never have surrendered, and these verses are in fact an emphasis on this point, so that his school of thought and practical method would become clear to all, and no one—friend or foe—would have such an expectation.

Therefore, the above phrase is a completion of the verse "And do not obey every habitual swearer, despicable." However, some have said this verse actually states the cause for the emergence of these traits, meaning: The arrogance resulting from wealth and numerous followers leads them to these moral vices. For this reason, all these traits are seen in many faithless wealthy and powerful people. However, the tone of the verses is more consistent with the first interpretation, which is why the majority of commentators have chosen it.

The next verse shows the reaction of such individuals, who possess these lowly traits, toward Divine verses. It says: "When Our verses are recited to him, he says, 'Legends of the former peoples!'" (إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا قالَ أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

With this excuse and this ugly label, he distances himself from Allah's verses, consigns them to oblivion, and seduces others as well. For this reason, one must not obey or follow such individuals, and this completes the prohibition of obeying such people.

The last verse under discussion reveals one of the punishments for this group, adding: "We will brand him upon the snout!" (سَنَسِمُهُ عَلَى الْخُرْطومِ). This is an eloquent and expressive description of their ultimate humiliation. Because: firstly, the use of the word "snout" (khurtum), which is only used for pigs and elephants, is a clear belittlement of them. Secondly, in the Arabic language, the "nose" is usually a metaphor for greatness and honor, just as in Persian, when we say "rub his nose in the dirt," it means to destroy his honor. Thirdly, branding is specific to animals; even among animals, they are not branded on the face, especially not on the nose, and this act is forbidden in Islam. All of this says with eloquent expression:

Allah will so humiliate such rebellious, self-centered, transgressing, and defiant individuals and sound the drum of their disgrace everywhere that they become a lesson for all.

The history of Islam also bears witness to this fact: this group of stubborn opponents became so wretched and humiliated with the progress of Islam that it was unparalleled, and the disgrace in the Hereafter is even greater.

Some commentators have said that most of the verses of this Surah refer to "Walid ibn al-Mughirah," one of the famous leaders of polytheism, but certainly, this does not prevent the generality of the verses' meaning and the breadth and inclusiveness of its expressions [1].

Key Points

1. Moral Vices

Although the above verses are a description of the vile traits of the fierce opponents of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), they simultaneously provide a model for identifying these traits—traits that distance man from Allah and cast him into the abyss of wretchedness and misery. These are traits that true believers must carefully guard against being tainted by. Therefore, many emphases have been made in Islamic narrations in this regard, including:

  1. In a hadith from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "Shall I not inform you of the most evil among you?" They said, "Yes, O Messenger of Allah!" He said: "Those who go about with slander, causing division between loved ones, and seeking faults in the innocent." [1] The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) specifically gave explicit advice in this regard, saying: "Let no one convey to me anything about any of my companions, for I love to come out to you with a sound heart." [2] Finally, in another hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that he said: "Three groups will not enter Paradise: the jawwaz, the ja'dhari, and the utull zanim." The narrator says I asked, "Who is the jawwaz?" He said: "Every hoarder who prevents [giving to others]." I asked, "Who is the ja'dhari?" He said: "Harsh and ill-tempered individuals." I asked, "Who is the utull zanim?" He said: "Gluttonous and ill-mannered individuals, those who eat and dress excessively and are oppressive and unjust." [3]

2. Compromise and Appeasement

One of the clear differences between the followers of the path of truth and political players is that the second group is not fixed on specific principles. Rather, they are always ready to give concessions in exchange for concessions they receive, and to abandon their principles for the sake of interests. Their creed and beliefs are not sacred to them, and they constantly bargain over them! This is exactly the content of the above verse which says: (وَدُّوا لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ): "They wish to draw you into their circle; just as they compromise and bargain, you too should compromise."

But the first group are never bargainers. They never lose their sacred goals for any price, and they do not bargain over them. Compromise (mudahanah), appeasement, and such political trade-offs have no place in their work. This is one of the best signs by which professional politicians can be recognized and distinguished from men of God.

In the famous sermon of Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام) in the Mosque of Damascus, it is also stated: "I am the son of the one who struck the snouts of the creation until they said 'There is no god but Allah'" (referring to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام)) (Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 45, p. 138). This expression, considering the verse under discussion where Allah says, "We will brand him upon the snout," has an interesting meaning and shows that the Divine Will was executed by the hand of His special servant, Ali (عليه السلام).

Ayahs

إِنَّا بَلَوْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا بَلَوْنَآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا۟ لَيَصْرِمُنَّهَا مُصْبِحِينَ (١٧)
Indeed we have tested them just as We tested the People of the Garden when they vowed they would gather its fruit at dawn, (17)
وَلَا يَسْتَثْنُونَ (١٨)
and they did not make any exception. (18)
فَطَافَ عَلَيْهَا طَآئِفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ (١٩)
Then, a visitation from your Lord visited it while they were asleep. (19)
فَأَصْبَحَتْ كَٱلصَّرِيمِ (٢٠)
So, by dawn it was like a harvested field. (20)
فَتَنَادَوْا۟ مُصْبِحِينَ (٢١)
At dawn they called out to one another, (21)
أَنِ ٱغْدُوا۟ عَلَىٰ حَرْثِكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰرِمِينَ (٢٢)
‘Get off early to your field if you have to gather [the fruits].’ (22)
فَٱنطَلَقُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَتَخَـٰفَتُونَ (٢٣)
So off they went, murmuring to one another: (23)
أَن لَّا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُم مِّسْكِينٌ (٢٤)
‘Today no needy man shall come to you in it.’ (24)
وَغَدَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ حَرْدٍ قَـٰدِرِينَ (٢٥)
And they set out at early morning, [considering themselves] able to deprive [the poor of its fruit]. (25)

Commentary

The Instructive Story of the "Owners of the Garden"

In connection with the previous verses regarding the self-centered and arrogant wealthy who, due to the abundance of wealth and children, turn their backs on everything, these verses mention a story about a group of wealthy people of the past who possessed a lush and green garden, and who were ultimately destroyed due to their obstinacy. This story appears to have been well-known among the people of that era, which is why it was cited as an example.

First, He says: "Indeed, We have tried them as We tried the owners of the garden" (إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ كَما بَلَوْنا أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ).

As for where this garden was located, there is discussion: was it in the land of Yemen near the great city of Sana'a? Or in the land of Ethiopia? Or among the Children of Israel in the land of Sham (Levant)? Or in Taif? However, the most famous view is that it was in Yemen.

The story was such that this garden was in the possession of a believing old man; he would take from it only as much as he needed and give the rest to the deserving and the needy. But when he passed away, his sons said: "We are more deserving of the harvest of this garden, for our dependents and children are many, and we cannot act as our father did!" Thus, they decided to deprive all the poor who used to benefit from it every year, and their fate became what we read in these verses:

It says: "We tried them when they swore that they would surely pluck the fruits of the garden in the morning, away from the sight of the needy" (إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا لَيَصْرِمُنَّها مُصْبِحِينَ) [1].

"And without making any exception," leaving nothing for the poor (وَ لا يَسْتَثْنُونَ). This decision of theirs shows that this act did not stem from need, but rather from stinginess and weakness of faith. For no matter how needy a person is, they can still allocate a small portion of the harvest of a high-yielding garden to the needy. Some have said that the meaning of "not making an exception" is that they did not say "unless Allah wills" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ); that is, they were so arrogant that they said, "We will go and do this," even seeing themselves as independent of saying "If Allah wills" (إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ).

[1]: "Yasrimunna" is from the root "Sarm" (rhyming with Sharm), meaning to pluck fruit, and it also means to cut off absolutely, as well as to make a matter firm.

However, the first interpretation is more correct [1].

Then, continuing this account, He adds: "So a visitation from your Lord circulated around it while they were asleep, and a pervasive punishment and calamity descended upon the entire garden while they were all in slumber" (فَطافَ عَلَيْها طائِفٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَ هُمْ نائِمُونَ).

A blazing fire and a deadly thunderbolt so overcame it that "that lush and green garden became like a black and dark night," and nothing remained of it but a handful of ashes (فَأَصْبَحَتْ كَالصَّرِيمِ). "Ta'if" is from the root "Tawaf," which originally means one who circles around something, but it is sometimes used as a metaphor for a calamity or disaster that occurs at night, and that is what is intended here.

"Sarim" is from the root "Sarm," meaning "to cut," and here it means "dark night," or "a tree without fruit," or "black ash." This is because the night is cut off by the arrival of the day, just as the day is cut off by the arrival of the night; hence, the night and day are sometimes called "al-Sariman." In any case, the meaning is that this heavenly calamity—which was apparently a massive thunderbolt—descended upon this orchard in such a way that it burned it all at once, leaving nothing but a handful of charcoal and black ash. And this is how thunderbolts are when they strike something.

[1]: Because, in addition to the specific suitability of the first meaning to the core of the story, if the second meaning were intended, it should have been said "wa lam tastathnu" instead of "wa la yastathnun" (pay close attention).

In any case, the owners of the garden, imagining that their fruitful trees were ready for harvesting, "called out to one another at early dawn" (فَتَنادَوْا مُصْبِحِينَ) [1].

And they said: "Go early to your tilth and orchard if you would pluck the fruits!" (أَنِ اغْدُوا عَلى‌ حَرْثِكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صارِمِينَ). "Ighdu" is from the root "Ghudwah," meaning the beginning of the day; hence, the food eaten at the start of the day (breakfast) is called "Ghada'" (although in current everyday Arabic usage, "Ghada'" refers to lunch).

Thus, "they departed, while they were whispering to one another" (فَانْطَلَقُوا وَ هُمْ يَتَخافَتُونَ).

"That, 'Let no poor person enter upon you today!'" (أَنْ لا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِسْكِينٌ). And they spoke so softly that their voices would not reach anyone's ears, lest a poor person find out and come to them for gleaning or to take a small amount of fruit to satisfy their hunger! It seems that because of the history of the father's good deeds, a group of the poor waited every year for such days when the harvesting of the garden would begin, so that a benefit might reach them. Therefore, these stingy and unfilial sons moved so secretly that no one would suspect such a day had arrived, so that by the time the poor found out, the matter would be finished.

And in this way, "they went early to their garden and tilth with full power to prevent the needy" (وَ غَدَوْا عَلى‌ حردٍ قادِرِينَ). "Hard" (rhyming with Sard) means prevention accompanied by intensity and anger. Yes, they were angry at the longing and expectation of the poor, and they were determined to prevent them with all their might (hence, this expression is used for years when the rain stops, or for a camel whose milk has also stopped).

Now let us see where their affair ultimately led:
