Ayahs

فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهَا قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لَضَآلُّونَ (٢٦)
But when they saw it, they said, ‘We have indeed lost our way!’ (26)
بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ (٢٧)
‘No, it is we who have been deprived!’ (27)
قَالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ لَوْلَا تُسَبِّحُونَ (٢٨)
The most upright among them said, ‘Did I not tell you, ‘‘Why do you not glorify [Allah]?’’ ’ (28)
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
They said, ‘Immaculate is our Lord! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (29)
فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَلَـٰوَمُونَ (٣٠)
Then they turned to one another, blaming each other. (30)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا طَـٰغِينَ (٣١)
They said, ‘Woe to us! Indeed, we have been rebellious. (31)
عَسَىٰ رَبُّنَآ أَن يُبْدِلَنَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهَآ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا رَٰغِبُونَ (٣٢)
Maybe our Lord will give us a better one in its place. Indeed we earnestly beseech our Lord.’ (32)
كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٣)
Such was their punishment; and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known. (33)

Commentary

The Painful End of the Owners of the Lush Garden

These verses continue the story of the "Owners of the Garden" (Aṣḥāb al-Jannah) mentioned in the preceding verses. The owners of the garden set out in the early morning, hoping to harvest their abundant crop, gather it away from the sight of the destitute, and keep it entirely for themselves—ensuring that not even a single poor person would sit at this vast table of Divine blessing. However, they were unaware that a deadly lightning bolt had turned the garden into a heap of ashes during the night, while they were asleep.

The Quran states: "When they saw the garden, its state was so ruined that they said: 'Indeed, we are lost!'" (فَلَمَّا رَأَوْها قالُوا إِنَّا لَضَالُّونَ).

The meaning of "lost" (ḍāllūn) might refer to losing the way to the garden, as mentioned above, or losing the path of truth, as some have suggested. However, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

Then they added: "Rather, we are [truly] deprived" (بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ). We intended to deprive the destitute and the needy, but we ourselves became the most deprived—deprived of both material income and the spiritual blessings that come through spending in the way of Allah and helping the needy.

"Among them, the most moderate (and wisest) of them said: 'Did I not say to you, why do you not glorify Allah?'" (قالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَ لَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ لَوْ لا تُسَبِّحُونَ). Did I not tell you to remember Allah in His greatness, to refrain from disobeying Him, to be grateful for His blessings, and to let the needy benefit from your wealth? But you did not listen, and you fell into this dark fate! It is inferred from this verse that there was a believer among them who forbade them from greed and stinginess, but because he was in the minority, no one listened to him. However, after this painful incident, his tongue was loosened, his logic became sharper and more piercing, and he subjected them to a barrage of blame and reproach.

They also woke up for a moment and confessed to their sin, saying: "Exalted is our Lord! Indeed, we were wrongdoers" (قالُوا سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ). We wronged both ourselves and others.

The term "awsaṭuhum" in the previous verse refers to the one who was at the peak of moderation in terms of intellect, wisdom, and knowledge. Some have interpreted it as being middle-aged, but this seems very unlikely, as there is no direct connection between age and uttering such profound words; the connection is between intellect and such speech.

The expression "لَوْ لا تُسَبِّحُونَ" (Why do you not glorify Allah?) is used because the root of all righteous deeds is faith, the knowledge of Allah (Ma'rifatullah), and the glorification and sanctification of Him.

Some have also interpreted "glorification" (tasbīḥ) here as gratitude for blessings, the necessary consequence of which is providing for the deprived. However, these two interpretations are not contradictory and are both encompassed within the concept of the verse.

As for their glorification before confessing to their sin, it may be because they wished to sanctify "Allah" from any injustice regarding this great calamity that befell their garden and destroyed it. They meant to say: "O Allah! It was we who wronged ourselves and others and deserved such a painful punishment, but Your action was pure justice and wisdom."

In some other verses of the Quran, this glorification is also seen before the admission of wrongdoing. As we read in the story of Yunus عليه السلام, when he was in the belly of the great fish, he said: "لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ" — "There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers" (Al-Anbiya 87).

Of course, "wrongdoing" (ẓulm) in the case of this great Prophet means "leaving the better choice" (tark al-awlā), as we explained in the commentary of that verse in Volume 13.

But the matter did not end there; "they turned to one another, blaming each other" (فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ يَتَلاوَمُونَ).

Likely, while each confessed to their own error, they also cast the primary blame upon one another, severely reproaching each other: "You were the main cause of our misfortune! Otherwise, we were not so estranged from God and justice!" Yes, such is the fate of all wrongdoers caught in the grip of Divine punishment; while admitting their sin, each tries to consider another as the primary cause of their misery. This is perhaps because in such cases, usually one proposes, another confirms, one undertakes the execution, and another expresses satisfaction through silence; yet it is clear that all of them are accomplices and involved in the sin.

Then it adds: When they realized the depth of their misery, they cried out and said: "Woe to us! Indeed, we were transgressors" (قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا طاغِينَ). In the previous stage, they confessed to "wrongdoing" (ẓulm), and here they confess to "transgression" (ṭughyān). In reality, transgression is a stage higher than wrongdoing, for a wrongdoer might accept the principle of the law but commit injustice due to the dominance of carnal desires, whereas a transgressor does not submit to the law at all and does not recognize it.

It is also possible that "wrongdoing" refers to "wronging oneself," while "transgression" refers to "violating the rights of others."

It is also noteworthy that when an Arab encounters a calamity or wishes to express loathing for something, they sometimes say "ways," sometimes "wayḥ," and sometimes "wayl." The first is for a light calamity, the second is more severe, and the third is the most intense. This shows that the owners of the garden considered themselves deserving of the severest reproach.

Finally, after this awakening, awareness, confession of sin, and return to Allah, they turned toward His threshold and said: "Perhaps our Lord will forgive our sins and give us in exchange a garden better than this one" (عَسى‌ رَبُّنا أَنْ يُبْدِلَنا خَيْراً مِنْها). "For indeed, we are turning to Him, placing our hearts in His Pure Essence, and seeking the solution to this problem from His infinite power."

(إِنَّا إِلى‌ رَبِّنا راغِبُونَ) [1] Did this group truly regret their actions, revise their ways, and make a firm decision that if they were granted Divine blessings in the future, they would fulfill the duty of gratitude? Or were they like many wrongdoers who wake up temporarily when caught in the storm of punishment, but as soon as the storm subsides, the same patterns are repeated?

There is discussion among commentators on this matter. What can perhaps be inferred from the tone of the following verse is that their repentance was not accepted because its conditions were not met. However, in some narrations, we read that they repented with sincere intention, and Allah accepted their repentance and granted them a better garden, which specifically had prolific grapevines with very large clusters.

In the final verse under discussion, as a general conclusion and a lesson for all, He says: "Such is the punishment [in this world], and the punishment of the Hereafter is greater, if they only knew" (كَذلِكَ الْعَذابُ وَ لَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ). You too, if you become intoxicated and arrogant due to wealth, property, and material means, and if the spirit of monopolization overcomes you—wanting everything for yourselves and depriving the needy—you will have no better fate. Except that one day "lightning" came and burned that garden; today, other calamities, and even devastating global or regional wars, may blow these blessings away.

Key Points

1. Monopolization: The Great Affliction of the Wealthy

Man naturally has an affection for worldly wealth because his livelihood depends on it, and this affection is not blameworthy within the bounds of moderation. What is important is to make the needy partners in your wealth; not only should one pay the obligatory Divine dues, but one should also not refrain from recommended acts of charity (infāq).

Specifically regarding gardens and agriculture, Islamic narrations instruct giving a portion to the needy present at the time, which—inspired by the noble verse "وَ آتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ" (And give its due on the day of its harvest) (Al-An'am 141)—is known as "Ḥaqq al-Ḥaṣād." This is a right other than the well-known Zakat, and it refers to what is given to the poor when they are present at the time of picking fruit or harvesting crops, and it has no fixed limit [1]. However, when the love for wealth appears in an extreme and deviant form, it takes the shape of monopolization, where sometimes, even without needing something himself, a person likes others to be deprived of it! In other words, he takes pleasure in Divine favors being exclusive to him. This is a great affliction that has many examples in human societies today and can be considered a type of dangerous disease.

The story of the "Owners of the Garden" mentioned in the verses above is a clear depiction of the monopolistic mindset of a group of wealthy people: how they join hands and plot to deprive the needy, harvesting the crop and taking massive profits away from their sight. But often, the sighs of these deprived people turn into burning lightning bolts that set fire to the harvest of the lives of these monopolistic wealthy individuals. It has often been seen that these lightning bolts appear in the form of revolutions, and they see with their own eyes what they did not believe; their cries reach the heavens and they speak of repentance and compensating for past errors, but it is too late.

2. The Relationship Between "Sin" and the "Cutting Off of Sustenance"

From the above story, it is also inferred that there is a close relationship between "sin" and the "cutting off of sustenance." Thus, in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"Indeed, a man commits a sin and sustenance is diverted from him," then the Imam عليه السلام recited these verses: "إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا لَيَصْرِمُنَّها مُصْبِحِينَ وَ لا يَسْتَثْنُونَ فَطافَ عَلَيْها طائِفٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَ هُمْ نائِمُونَ"

"When the owners of the garden swore that they would harvest the fruits in the morning and not allow even one person other than them to benefit, but a calamity from your Lord overcame that garden while they were asleep and destroyed it." [1] It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that "the relationship between sin and the cutting off of sustenance is clearer than the sun, as Allah has explained it in Surah Nun wal-Qalam (the Surah under discussion)!" [2]

[1]: "Rāghibūn" is from the root "raghbah." When this root is transitive with "ilā" or "fī," it means inclination toward something, and when it is transitive with "‘an," it means turning away from or indifference toward something.

[1]: For narrations related to this subject, please see Volume 6 of Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Chapters on Zakat of Crops, Chapter 13, and Sunan al-Bayhaqi, Volume 4, page 133.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 395 (Hadith 44).

[2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 37.

Ayahs

إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٣٤)
Indeed for the Godwary there will be gardens of bliss near their Lord. (34)
أَفَنَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ كَٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٣٥)
Shall We treat those who submit [to Us] as [We treat] the guilty? (35)
مَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ (٣٦)
What is the matter with you? How do you judge! (36)
أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ فِيهِ تَدْرُسُونَ (٣٧)
Do you possess a scripture in which you read (37)
إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِيهِ لَمَا تَخَيَّرُونَ (٣٨)
that you shall have in it whatever you choose? (38)
أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمَـٰنٌ عَلَيْنَا بَـٰلِغَةٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۙ إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَمَا تَحْكُمُونَ (٣٩)
Do you have a pledge binding on Us until the Day of Resurrection, that you shall indeed have whatever you decide? (39)
سَلْهُمْ أَيُّهُم بِذَٰلِكَ زَعِيمٌ (٤٠)
Ask them, which of them will aver [any of] that! (40)
أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا۟ بِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤١)
Do they have any ‘partners’ [that they claim for Allah]? Then let them produce their partners, if they are truthful. (41)

Commentary

1- A Comprehensive Interrogation

We know that the method of the Quran is to place the life stories of the wicked and the righteous in contrast with one another, so that they may be better understood through comparison; this method is highly effective from an educational and pedagogical perspective.

Following this very method, after mentioning the painful fate of the "Companions of the Garden" (the owners of the lush green orchard) in the preceding verses, it proceeds to describe the state of the pious, saying:

"Indeed, for the righteous with their Lord are the Gardens of Pleasure" (إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ).

These are gardens of Paradise where every conceivable blessing exists in its most perfect form, in addition to blessings that have never even occurred to the mind of any human being.

However, since there were groups among the polytheists and the wealthy who were so self-centered that they claimed, "Just as our condition is excellent in this world, it will also be very good in the Resurrection," Allah severely reprimands them in the following verse—or rather, He puts them on trial—saying: "Should We then treat the Muslims (those who submit to truth and justice) like the criminals (the polytheists)?" (أَ فَنَجْعَلُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ كَالْمُجْرِمِينَ).

"What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?!" (ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ). Would any rational person believe that the fate of the just and the oppressor, the obedient and the criminal, the selfless and the monopolizer, could be the same? Especially before the presence of a Lord whose every action is based on a wise plan and reckoning.

In Surah Fussilat (Verse 50), a reference is also made to such individuals, where He says: (وَ لَئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي وَ ما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَ لَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى‌ رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى‌):

"And if We let him taste mercy from Us after an adversity which has touched him, he will surely say, 'This is [due] to me (my own merit), and I do not think the Hour will occur; and even if I should be returned to my Lord, indeed, for me with Him is the best [reward].'" Yes, this arrogant and self-satisfied group considers both this world and the hereafter to belong to them!

Then He adds: "If your intellect and reason have not guided you to such a judgment, do you have any evidence from 'tradition' (revelation)? Do you have a book in which you study?" (أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتابٌ فِيهِ تَدْرُسُونَ).

"That indeed you shall have in it whatever you choose and desire?" (إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِيهِ لَما تَخَيَّرُونَ) [1]. You expect that criminals like yourselves should be on the same level as the Muslims; this is a claim that neither reason dictates nor is found in any credible book.

In the next verse, He continues: "If you have no evidence from reason or revelation for this claim, do you have binding oaths from Us, reaching until the Day of Resurrection, that you shall have whatever you judge [for yourselves]?" (أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمانٌ عَلَيْنا بالِغَةٌ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَما تَحْكُمُونَ).

Who can claim to have taken a covenant and oath from Allah that He will submit to their desires and grant them every privilege and status they want without question?! To the extent that criminals would be ranked alongside believers [2].

Continuing these questions that block their paths from every direction, He adds: "Ask them which of them will guarantee that [the criminals and believers will be equal, or that Allah will provide them with whatever they desire]" (سَلْهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ بِذلِكَ زَعِيمٌ).

And in the final stage of this extraordinary interrogation, He says: "Or do they have 'partners' (deities) who will intercede for them and support them before Allah? Then let them bring their partners, if they are truthful!" (أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا بِشُرَكائِهِمْ إِنْ كانُوا صادِقِينَ).

Do they have the slightest evidence that these idols—these low-value, mindless inanimate objects—are partners of Allah and intercessors at His threshold?

Some commentators have interpreted "partners" (shuraka) here to mean "witnesses" (shuhada).

Thus, in a summary of the above verses, it can be concluded that to prove their claim—that they are on par with the believers, or even superior to them—they must cling to one of four means: either a proof from reason, or a book from the heavenly scriptures, or a covenant and oath from Allah, or the intercession of intercessors and the testimony of witnesses. Since the answer to all these questions is negative, the aforementioned claim is entirely baseless and worthless.

[^1]: The phrase "Inna lakum..." is the object (maf'ul) for "tadrusun," and normally "anna" (with a fatha on the hamza) should be read, but due to the "lam" appearing at the beginning of the noun of "inna," it is read as "inna" (with a kasra), as the verb is suspended from acting directly.

[^2]: Some have interpreted the word "balighah" here to mean "emphasized/binding," and others as "continuous/extending." The second meaning is more appropriate; therefore, the prepositional phrase "until the Day of Resurrection" (ila yawm al-qiyamah) relates to it.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (٤٢)
The day when the catastrophe occurs and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, they will not be able [to do it]. (42)
خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ وَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سَـٰلِمُونَ (٤٣)
With a humbled look [in their eyes], they will be overcast by abasement. Certainly, they were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet sound. (43)
فَذَرْنِى وَمَن يُكَذِّبُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ ۖ سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٤)
So leave Me with those who deny this discourse. We will draw them imperceptibly [into ruin], whence they do not know. (44)
وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ (٤٥)
I will grant them respite, for My devising is indeed sure. (45)

Commentary

That Day They Will Want to Prostrate but Will Not Be Able

Following the previous verses which subjected the polytheists and criminals to a crushing interrogation, the verses under discussion depict a portion of their fate on the Day of Resurrection, so it may become clear how humiliated and debased this self-centered and pretentious group will be on that day.

It says: "Remember the day when the shin shall be laid bare out of intense fear and horror, and they shall be summoned to prostrate, but they shall not be able to do so" (يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ) [^1]. The phrase "يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ" (the shins shall be bared) is, according to a group of commentators, a metaphor for the intensity of horror, terror, and the gravity of the situation. This is because it was customary among the Arabs that when faced with a difficult task, they would tuck up their skirts and bare their shins. Therefore, we read that when the famous commentator Ibn Abbas was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said: "Whenever something from the Quran is hidden from you, refer to the expressions of the Arabs in poetry; have you not heard the poet say:

And the war stood us upon the shin (wa qamat al-harbu bina 'ala saqin)—meaning the intensity of the war's crisis."

Some have also said that "saq" (shin/stalk) means the root and foundation of something, like the stalk of a tree. Therefore, "يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ" means that on that day, the roots of everything will be revealed. However, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

Indeed, on that day, everyone will be invited to prostrate and show humility before the Lord. The believers will fall into prostration, but the criminals will lack the power to prostrate! This is because the ugly dispositions that had become ingrained in their being in the world will manifest on that day and prevent them from bending their backs before the Pure Essence of Allah.

Here a question arises: the Day of Resurrection is not a day of religious obligation (taklif), so what is the purpose of the summons to prostrate?

The answer to this question can be derived from the expressions found in the hadiths. In a narration, we read: "On the Day of Resurrection, a veil of Divine Light will be lifted, and the believers will fall into prostration due to its greatness, but the backs of the hypocrites will become so stiff that they will not have the power to prostrate!" [^1] In other words: on that day, the greatness of Allah will become manifest; this greatness invites the believers to prostrate and they fall into prostration, but the disbelievers are deprived of this felicity.

[^1]: "Yawm" (Day) is an adverb of time (zarf) and relates to a deleted verb, estimated as "Remember the day..." (idhkuru yawma...). Some have also considered the possibility that it relates to "falyatu" (let them bring) in the previous verse, but this meaning seems remote.

The next verse says: "This is while their eyes are cast down from intense regret and shame, and ignominy covers them" (خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ) [^2]. When criminals are condemned in court, they usually cast their heads down, and humiliation envelops their entire being.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 395, Hadith 49. [^2]: "Tarhaquhum" is from the root "rahaq" (rhyming with shafaq), meaning to cover or envelop.

Then it adds: "They had been summoned to prostrate before this in the life of the world while they were healthy" (وَ قَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَ هُمْ سالِمُونَ).

But they never prostrated, and they brought the spirit of arrogance, rebellion, and defiance with them to the scene of Resurrection; under such circumstances, how could they have the power to prostrate?

It is clear that the summons to prostrate in the world took place through the call of the muezzins at the time of prayer, through congregational prayer gatherings, and through the verses of the Quran and the hadiths of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the Infallible Imams (peace be upon them). This summons has a broad and expansive meaning that includes all of these.

Then, addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), He says: "Leave Me with those who deny this discourse (meaning the Quran) so that I may settle the account with all of them!" (فَذَرْنِي وَ مَنْ يُكَذِّبُ بِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ). This is a severe threat from the Almighty, All-Powerful Allah, who tells the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "There is no need for you to interfere; leave Me with these stubborn and rebellious deniers, so that I may give them what they deserve!" Let us not forget that this is said by Allah, who is Powerful over all things. This expression also serves as a source of consolation and heartening for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the believers in the face of the obstructions and conspiracies of the enemies. Then He adds: "We shall lead them on gradually [to their ruin] from where they do not know" (سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

"And I give them respite, and I will not hasten their punishment, for My plan is firm and precise, and My punishment is severe" (وَ أُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ).

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "When a servant commits a sin, Allah renews a blessing for him, so he abandons seeking forgiveness; this is the 'istidraj' (gradual leading to ruin)." [^1] From this hadith and others narrated in this regard, it is understood that sometimes, to punish antagonistic and stubborn servants, Allah grants them blessings in response to the sins they commit. They imagine this is Divine grace bestowed upon them due to their merit! Consequently, they sink into pride and negligence, but suddenly Allah seizes them and plunges them from the midst of luxury and blessings into the jaws of punishment and calamity—and this is the most painful form of punishment.

Of course, this applies to those who have reached the peak of rebellion and defiance. However, for individuals who have not yet gone that far, Allah disciplines them in response to their sins, and that becomes the cause of their awakening and repentance; this is Allah's grace toward them.

In other words: when a person sins, they are in one of three states: Either they realize it themselves and return, or Allah strikes them with the whip of "calamity" so they wake up, or they are worthy of neither of these. In place of calamity, Allah grants them blessings, and this is the "punishment of istidraj" which is mentioned in the verses of the Quran either

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 340.

by this very term or by other expressions.

Therefore, a person must be cautious when Divine blessings come their way, lest this matter—which appears to be a blessing—becomes the "punishment of istidraj." For this reason, awakened Muslims would fall into thought at such times and review their actions. As mentioned in a hadith, one of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "I asked Allah for wealth and He provided for me; I asked for a child and He granted me one; I asked for a house and He bestowed it upon me. I fear that this might be 'istidraj'!" The Imam replied: "If these are accompanied by the praise and gratitude of Allah, it is not istidraj (it is a blessing)." [^1] The expression "أُمْلِي لَهُمْ" (I give them respite) indicates that Allah never hastens in punishing the oppressors. One who hastens is one who fears that opportunities will slip away, but for Allah the Almighty, who brings about whatever He wills at any moment, the loss of opportunity has no meaning. In any case, this is a warning to all oppressors and tyrants that health, blessings, and security should never make them arrogant, and they should expect Allah's severe retribution at any moment. [^2]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 397. [^2]: Regarding the punishment of "istidraj," we provided a more detailed explanation in Vol. 7, p. 32 onwards (under verse 182 of Surah al-A'raf), and also in Vol. 3, p. 182 under verse 178 of Surah Al-Imran.

Ayahs

أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ أَجْرًا فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ (٤٦)
Do you ask them for a reward, so that they are weighed down with debt? (46)
أَمْ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ (٤٧)
Do they possess [access to] the Unseen, so that they write it down? (47)
فَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تَكُن كَصَاحِبِ ٱلْحُوتِ إِذْ نَادَىٰ وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ (٤٨)
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, and do not be like the Man of the Fish who called out as he choked with grief. (48)
لَّوْلَآ أَن تَدَٰرَكَهُۥ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ لَنُبِذَ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ (٤٩)
Had it not been for a blessing that came to his rescue from his Lord, he would surely have been cast on the bare shore, being blameworthy. (49)
فَٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَجَعَلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٥٠)
So his Lord chose him and made him one of the righteous. (50)

Commentary

Do Not Hasten in Requesting Punishment

Following the interrogations of the polytheists and criminals in the previous verses, these verses add two more questions. First, it says: "Or is it that you ask them for a payment, so they are burdened by a debt?" (أَمْ تَسْئَلُهُمْ أَجْراً فَهُمْ مِنْ مَغْرَمٍ مُثْقَلُونَ).

If their excuse is that listening to your invitation costs money and they must pay a heavy price for it, which they are unable to afford, this is a lie. You do not demand any reward or payment from them at all, nor did any other prophet among the prophets of Allah demand a wage.

"Maghram" (مغرم) is derived from the root "gharamat" (غرامت), meaning a loss that befalls a person without them having committed a crime or treachery. "Muthqal" (مثقل) is from the root "thiql" (ثقل), meaning heaviness. In this way, another excuse is taken away from the excuse-seekers.

The above verse, and the verse following it, appear exactly the same in Surah At-Tur (verses 40-41).

Then, continuing this discourse, it adds: "Or is the Unseen with them, so they write [it] down?" (أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ). This is, in fact, one of the remote possibilities that the disbelievers might cling to, and the Quran has not overlooked it. It suggests they might claim to be connected to the world of the Unseen through soothsayers and the like, thereby receiving the secrets of the Unseen, writing them down, and passing them to one another. Through this, they might claim to have discovered their superiority over the Muslims, or at least their equality with them.

Certainly, they had no evidence for such a claim, and this sentence carries the meaning of a rhetorical denial (Istifham Inkari). The suggestion by some that "the Unseen" refers to the Preserved Tablet (Lawh al-Mahfuz) and "writing" refers to predestination and decree seems very unlikely, as they never claimed that the matter of destiny and the Preserved Tablet was in their hands.

Since the stubbornness and irrationality of the polytheists and enemies of Islam sometimes so distressed the heart of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he might have been inclined to invoke a curse upon them, Allah comforts His Prophet in the next verse and commands him to be patient and steadfast, saying: "So be patient for the decision of your Lord" (فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ).

Wait until Allah provides the means for your victory and that of your companions, and the defeat of your enemies. Never hasten for their punishment, and know that these respites given to them are a form of gradual punishment (Istidraj).

Therefore, the meaning of "the decision of your Lord" (لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ) is Allah's final decree regarding the victory of the Muslims. However, some have said it means: be patient and steadfast in the path of conveying the commands of your Lord.

There is also the possibility that it means: since your Lord has decreed it, be patient [1]. But the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Then He adds: "And be not like the companion of the fish (Jonah) who..."

[1]: In this case, the "lam" in "li-hukmi rabbika" is the "lam" of causality (lam al-ta'lil).

"...hastened for the punishment of his people and was afflicted with the consequence of 'leaving the better way' (tark al-awla)" (وَ لا تَكُنْ كَصاحِبِ الْحُوتِ).

"When he called out while he was distressed [in the belly of the fish]" (إِذْ نادى‌ وَ هُوَ مَكْظُومٌ).

The "call" (nida) referred to here is the same mentioned in verse 87 of Surah Al-Anbiya: فَنادى‌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ ("And he called out within the darknesses, 'There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers'"). Thus, he confessed to his "leaving the better way" and requested pardon and forgiveness from Allah.

There is also a possibility that "call" refers to the curse he invoked upon his people while he was full of rage and anger, but commentators have preferred the first interpretation because the expression "nadi" (called out) in this verse harmonizes with the expression in verse 87 of Surah Al-Anbiya, which certainly pertains to the time he was imprisoned in the belly of the fish.

In any case, "makzum" (مكظوم) is derived from the root "kazm" (کظم), meaning the throat. "Kazm al-siqa" means tying the mouth of a water-skin after it is full. Accordingly, those who become very angry or sorrowful but restrain themselves are called "kazim" (کاظم). For the same reason, this word is also used in the sense of "imprisonment." Therefore, "makzum" in the above verse can have two meanings: being full of rage and sorrow, or being imprisoned in the belly of the fish. However, the first meaning, as we said, is more appropriate.

In the next verse, He adds: "Had not a favor from his Lord overtaken him, he would have been cast out onto the naked shore while he was censured" (لَوْ لا أَنْ تَدارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ لَنُبِذَ بِالْعَراءِ وَ هُوَ مَذْمُومٌ) [1]. We know that eventually, Jonah was cast out from the belly of the fish onto a dry wilderness, which the Quran refers to as "ara" (عراء). However, this happened while Allah had accepted his repentance and included him in His mercy, so he was never deserving of censure.

In Surah As-Saffat, verses 145 and 146, we also read: فَنَبَذْناهُ بِالْعَراءِ وَ هُوَ سَقِيمٌ وَ أَنْبَتْنا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِنْ يَقْطِينٍ ("So We cast him onto the open shore while he was ill. And We caused to grow over him a gourd vine")—so that he might rest under the shade of its wide, moist leaves. Apparently, the "favor" (ni'mah) in the verse under discussion refers to the success of repentance and being encompassed by divine mercy.

Two questions arise here: First, in verses 143 and 144 of Surah As-Saffat, it says: فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ ("And had he not been of those who exalt Allah, he would have remained in its belly until the Day they are resurrected"). This seems to contradict what is mentioned in the verse under discussion.

In response to this objection, it can be said that two different punishments—one more severe and the other lighter—awaited Jonah. The first was to remain in the belly of the fish until the end of the world, which was averted by the blessing of his glorification and praise of Allah. The second was that when he emerged from the belly of the fish, he would be censured and far from Allah's grace; this too was averted by the blessing of his Lord's favor and His special mercy.

[1]: Although "ni'mah" (نعمت) is feminine, its verb (tadaraka) appears in the masculine form. This is because the feminine subject is figurative (lafzi) and the object pronoun separates the verb and the subject (take note).

The second question: In verse 142 of Surah As-Saffat, it says: فَالْتَقَمَهُ الْحُوتُ وَ هُوَ مُلِيمٌ ("Then the fish swallowed him, while he was blameworthy"). Yet, from the verse under discussion, it is understood that there was no censure or blame upon him.

The answer to this question also becomes clear by noting one point: this blame (mulim) pertains to the time he was first placed in the belly of the fish, whereas the removal of censure pertains to the time he repented, Allah accepted his repentance, and he was delivered from the belly of the fish.

Therefore, in the next verse, He says: "But his Lord chose him and made him of the righteous" (فَاجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَجَعَلَهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ). Following that, He once again entrusted him with the mission of guiding his people. He returned to them, and they all believed, and Allah allowed them to enjoy the provisions of life for a long time.

The detailed account of Jonah and his people, as well as other issues related to his "leaving the better way," his placement in the belly of the fish, and various questions raised in this regard, have been provided in detail in Volume 19, pages 152 to 168 (under verses 139 to 148 of Surah As-Saffat), and also in Volume 13, pages 484 to 489 (under verses 87 and 88 of Surah Al-Anbiya).

Ayahs

وَإِن يَكَادُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ لَمَّا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُۥ لَمَجْنُونٌ (٥١)
Indeed the faithless almost devour you with their eyes when they hear this Reminder, and they say, ‘He is indeed crazy.’ (51)
وَمَا هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٥٢)
Yet it is just a reminder for all the nations. (52)

Commentary

They Want to Destroy You, But They Cannot

The two preceding verses, which form the conclusion of Surah "Al-Qalam," are in fact a follow-up to what was mentioned at the beginning of this Surah regarding the enemies' accusation of madness against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

First, He says: "And indeed, those who disbelieve would almost make you slip with their eyes when they hear the Message, and they say: 'Indeed, he is mad'" (وَ إِنْ يَكادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصارِهِمْ لَمَّا سَمِعُوا الذِّكْرَ وَ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُ لَمَجْنُونٌ).

"لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ" is derived from the root "zalaq," meaning to slip and fall to the ground, and it is a metaphor for destruction and annihilation.

Various opinions have been offered in the commentary of this verse:

1- Many commentators have said: The meaning is that when the enemies hear the magnificent verses of the Quran from you, they become so enraged and distressed, and look upon you with such enmity, as if they want to cast you to the ground and destroy you with their eyes! In explaining this meaning, a group has added that they want to eliminate you through the "evil eye," which many people believe in, saying that in some eyes there lies a mysterious effect that can make the target ill or destroy them with a specific look.

2- Others have said that this is a metaphor for extremely wrathful looks, similar to when we say, "So-and-so looked at me so badly as if he wanted to eat me or kill me with his gaze!"

3- Another interpretation for this verse comes to mind, which might be closer than the above interpretations: the Quran wants to reveal the strange contradiction that existed within the statements of the enemies of Islam. That is: when they hear the verses of the Quran, they become so captivated and express such wonderment at it that they want to cast an "evil eye" upon you (since the evil eye is usually cast upon things that are highly admirable), yet at the same time, they say: "You are mad." This is truly astonishing—where is madness and incoherent speech, and where are these admirable, attractive, and influential verses?

These shallow-minded people do not know what they are saying or what contradictory labels they are attributing to you.

In any case, as to whether the "evil eye" is a reality from the Islamic perspective and from the viewpoint of modern sciences, we will speak in the "Key Points" section, Allah willing.

And finally, in the last verse, He adds: "But it is nothing less than a Reminder to the worlds" (وَ ما هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ). Its knowledge is enlightening, its warnings are awakening, its parables are meaningful, its encouragements and glad tidings are soul-nurturing, and as a whole, it is a source of awakening for the sleepers and a reminder for the heedless. Given this, how can the accusation of madness be given to the one who brought it?

According to this interpretation, "dhikr" (on the pattern of fikr) here means "reminder," but some commentators have interpreted it as "honor," saying this Quran is an honor for all the worlds, similar to what appears in verse 44 of Surah Az-Zukhruf which says: وَ إِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَ لِقَوْمِكَ "And indeed, it is a reminder (honor) for you and your people." However, as we mentioned under the aforementioned verse, "dhikr" there also means reminder and providing awareness, and fundamentally, one of the names of the Holy Quran is "Ad-Dhikr." Therefore, the first interpretation seems more correct.

Key Point: Is the Evil Eye a Reality?

Many people believe that in some eyes there is a special effect such that when they look at something with admiration, it may destroy it or break it, and if it is a human, make them ill or mad.

From a rational perspective, this matter is not an impossibility. Many modern scientists believe that in some eyes, a specific magnetic force is hidden which has great efficacy; it can even be developed through practice and exercise. Hypnosis is achieved through this very magnetic force of the eyes.

In a world where the "laser beam"—which is an invisible ray—can do things that no destructive weapon can achieve, accepting the existence of a force in some eyes that affects the opposite party through specific waves would not be a strange thing.

Many report that they have seen with their own eyes individuals who possessed this mysterious power of the eye, and have disabled people, animals, or objects through the evil eye.

Therefore, not only should one not insist on denying these matters, but one must accept the possibility of its existence from the perspective of reason and science.

In Islamic narrations, various expressions are also seen that briefly confirm the existence of such a thing.

In a hadith, we read that "Asma bint Umays" said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Sometimes the children of Ja'far are affected by the evil eye; shall I obtain a 'ruqyah' for them?" (By "ruqyah" is meant prayers that are written and kept by individuals to prevent the evil eye, also called a "ta'widh" or amulet).

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

نعم، فلو كان شى‌ء يسبق القدر لسبقه العين

"Yes, there is no harm in it; if anything could precede the Divine Decree (Qadar), it would be the evil eye!" [1]

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 341.

In another hadith, it is mentioned that the Commander of the Faithful علیه السلام said: The Prophet took a "ruqyah" for Imam Hasan and Imam Husayn علیهما السلام, and recited this prayer:

اعيذكما بكلمات التامة و اسماء اللَّه الحسنى كلها عامة، من شر السامة و الهامة، و من شر كل عين لامة، و من شر حاسد اذا حسد

"I commend you to all the perfect words and the Beautiful Names of Allah, all of them entirely, from the evil of death and venomous pests, and from the evil of every stinging eye, and from the evil of the envier when he envies." Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله looked at us and said: "This is how Prophet Ibrahim sought protection for Isma'il and Ishaq." [1] It is also mentioned in Nahj al-Balagha:

العين حق و الرقى حق

"The evil eye is a reality, and resorting to prayer to repel it is also a reality." [2] It is necessary to mention this point: there is no obstacle to these prayers and supplications, by Allah's command, preventing the effect of the mysterious magnetic force of the eyes, just as prayers affect many other destructive factors and neutralize them by Allah's command.

It is also necessary to remind that accepting the effect of the evil eye in general does not mean resorting to superstitious acts and vulgar practices in such cases, which are both contrary to the instructions of the Sharia and a cause for doubt and hesitation for uninformed individuals regarding the core subject, just as the contamination of many truths with superstitions has left this undesirable impression in minds.

[1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 400.

[2]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, No. 400 (This hadith is also narrated in Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 7, p. 171, Chapter: The Evil Eye is a Reality, in this form: العين حق). In "Al-Mu'jam al-Mufahras li-Alfaz al-Hadith al-Nabawi," this same meaning is narrated from various sources (Vol. 4, p. 451).

O Allah! Keep us in Your protection from the evil of the wicked and the plots of the enemies.

O Lord! Grant us steadfastness and patience through which we may earn Your pleasure.

O God! Grant us the success to benefit from Your endless blessings before ingratitude strips them from us.

Amin, O Lord of the Worlds. "End of Surah Al-Qalam" 28 Shawwal al-Mukarram 1406.
